examples.explanation: Result size = 3 + 2 - 1 = 4 coefficients (degrees 0-3). Initialize result = [0,0,0,0]. i=0 (coeff 1): j=0 (coeff 4): product=4, result[0+0]=4. j=1 (coeff 5): product=5, result[0+1]=5. i=1 (coeff 2): j=0: product=8, result[1+0]=5+8=13. j=1: product=10, result[1+1]=0+10=10. i=2 (coeff 3): j=0: product=12, result[2+0]=10+12=22. j=1: product=15, result[2+1]=0+15=15. Final result = [4,13,22,15] (all mod 1000000007, but no reductions needed as values are small).

Result size = 3 + 2 - 1 = 4 coefficients. Initialize result = [0,0,0,0]. i=0 (coeff 2): j=0 (coeff 3): product=6, result[0]=6. j=1 (coeff 4): product=8 mod 7 = 1, result[1]=1. i=1 (coeff 0): j=0: product=0, result[1]=1+0=1. j=1: product=0, result[2]=0. i=2 (coeff 1): j=0: product=3, result[2]=0+3=3. j=1: product=4, result[3]=4. Final result = [6,1,3,4] representing 6 + x + 3x² + 4x³.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: Directly multiply polynomials using nested loops to compute each coefficient as the sum of products of terms from the input polynomials, then reduce each coefficient modulo the prime. This is straightforward but inefficient for large degrees.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Polynomial Multiplication Mod Prime
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of polynomial multiplication using nested loops with modulo reduction.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete solution to multiply two polynomials modulo a prime using the brute force O(n²) approach. The intuition is straightforward: each coefficient in the product polynomial is the sum of products of coefficients from the input polynomials whose exponents add up to that coefficient's exponent. We use nested loops to iterate through all possible pairs of terms from the two polynomials, compute their product, and add it to the appropriate position in the result array. Since we're working modulo a prime, we must reduce each intermediate sum modulo the prime to prevent overflow and ensure correctness. The algorithm: 1) Determine the size of the result array (degree of poly1 + degree of poly2 + 1). 2) Initialize result array with zeros. 3) For each coefficient i in poly1 and each coefficient j in poly2, compute product = (poly1[i] * poly2[j]) % prime, then add it to result[i+j] modulo prime. 4) Return the result array. Time complexity is O(n*m) where n and m are the degrees of the input polynomials plus 1. Space complexity is O(n+m) for the result array. This connects directly to the key subroutine (L1) which is the core multiplication loop. Common mistakes include forgetting to initialize the result array properly, not handling modulo reduction correctly (especially for negative numbers in some languages), and off-by-one errors in array indices. The brute force approach is inefficient for large polynomials but serves as a foundation for understanding more advanced algorithms like NTT.

examples.trace: Result size = 3 + 2 - 1 = 4 coefficients (degrees 0-3). Initialize result = [0,0,0,0]. i=0 (coeff 1): j=0 (coeff 4): product=4, result[0+0]=4. j=1 (coeff 5): product=5, result[0+1]=5. i=1 (coeff 2): j=0: product=8, result[1+0]=5+8=13. j=1: product=10, result[1+1]=0+10=10. i=2 (coeff 3): j=0: product=12, result[2+0]=10+12=22. j=1: product=15, result[2+1]=0+15=15. Final result = [4,13,22,15] (all mod 1000000007, but no reductions needed as values are small).

Result size = 3 + 2 - 1 = 4 coefficients. Initialize result = [0,0,0,0]. i=0 (coeff 2): j=0 (coeff 3): product=6, result[0]=6. j=1 (coeff 4): product=8 mod 7 = 1, result[1]=1. i=1 (coeff 0): j=0: product=0, result[1]=1+0=1. j=1: product=0, result[2]=0. i=2 (coeff 1): j=0: product=3, result[2]=0+3=3. j=1: product=4, result[3]=4. Final result = [6,1,3,4] representing 6 + x + 3x² + 4x³.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Polynomial Coefficient Multiplication Loop
ladders.desc: The nested loop that computes each coefficient of the product polynomial.
ladders.explanation: This is the most important helper function: the nested loop structure that multiplies coefficients from two polynomials and accumulates results. The intuition is that to compute coefficient k in the product polynomial, we need to sum all products poly1[i] * poly2[j] where i+j = k. The algorithm uses two nested loops: outer loop iterates through indices i of poly1, inner loop iterates through indices j of poly2. For each pair (i,j), we compute product = (poly1[i] * poly2[j]) % prime and add it to result[i+j] modulo prime. Time complexity is O(n*m) where n and m are the lengths of the input arrays. Space complexity is O(1) extra space beyond the result array. This feeds directly into the full problem (L0) as the core computational step. Common mistakes include incorrect array bounds (result array should have size n+m-1), forgetting to use modulo operation on the product or sum, and inefficient double modulo operations. The loop structure is simple but forms the basis for understanding why brute force is O(n²) and how more advanced algorithms optimize this.

examples.input: poly1 = [1, 2], poly2 = [3, 4], result array initialized to [0,0,0,0], prime = 1000000007
examples.trace: Initialize result = [0,0,0,0]. i=0 (coeff 1): j=0 (coeff 3): product=3, result[0]=0+3=3. j=1 (coeff 4): product=4, result[1]=0+4=4. i=1 (coeff 2): j=0: product=6, result[1]=4+6=10. j=1: product=8, result[2]=0+8=8. Final result = [3,10,8,0] (degree 3 coefficient is 0).

examples.input: poly1 = [2, 0, 1], poly2 = [1, 1], result array initialized to [0,0,0,0,0], prime = 5
examples.trace: Initialize result = [0,0,0,0,0]. i=0 (coeff 2): j=0 (coeff 1): product=2, result[0]=2. j=1 (coeff 1): product=2, result[1]=2. i=1 (coeff 0): j=0: product=0, result[1]=2+0=2. j=1: product=0, result[2]=0. i=2 (coeff 1): j=0: product=1, result[2]=0+1=1. j=1: product=1, result[3]=1. Final result = [2,2,1,1,0].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Coefficient Accumulation with Modular Arithmetic
ladders.desc: How to combine products of coefficients while maintaining modulo prime correctness.
ladders.explanation: The central insight is how to correctly compute each coefficient of the product polynomial as a sum of products modulo prime. For coefficient k in the result, we need result[k] = Σ (poly1[i] * poly2[j]) mod prime for all i,j where i+j=k. The key technique is to perform modular reduction at each step to prevent overflow and ensure mathematical correctness. We compute each product modulo prime, then add it to the current result[k] modulo prime. This is crucial because working with large polynomials and primes (like 1e9+7) can cause intermediate values to exceed standard integer limits. The algorithm: For each pair (i,j), compute temp = (poly1[i] * poly2[j]) % prime, then result[i+j] = (result[i+j] + temp) % prime. Time complexity per operation is O(1), but overall O(n*m) for all pairs. Space is O(1) per coefficient. This connects to the key subroutine (L1) as the fundamental operation within the loops. Common mistakes include delaying modulo reduction until the end (risking overflow), incorrect handling of negative numbers (in languages where % can be negative), and not understanding that (a*b) mod p = ((a mod p)*(b mod p)) mod p. This modular accumulation is essential for correctness in cryptographic and number-theoretic applications.

examples.input: Compute coefficient 2 for poly1=[1,2,3], poly2=[4,5], prime=7
examples.trace: Coefficient 2 comes from pairs where i+j=2: (i=0,j=2) invalid since j max is 1, (i=1,j=1): 2*5=10 mod 7=3, (i=2,j=0): 3*4=12 mod 7=5. Sum = 3+5=8 mod 7=1. So result[2]=1.

examples.input: Compute coefficient 1 for poly1=[1000000006,1], poly2=[2], prime=1000000007
examples.trace: Coefficient 1 comes from pairs where i+j=1: (i=0,j=1) invalid since j max is 0, (i=1,j=0): 1*2=2 mod 1000000007=2. So result[1]=2.

ladders.title: Building Block — Modular Multiplication of Two Integers
ladders.desc: Computing (a * b) mod prime correctly and efficiently.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is computing the product of two integers modulo a prime. The intuition is that we need to prevent overflow while maintaining mathematical correctness: (a * b) mod p = ((a mod p) * (b mod p)) mod p. This property allows us to reduce numbers modulo p before multiplication, which is crucial when working with large numbers (like near 1e9). The algorithm: 1) Take inputs a and b and prime p. 2) Compute a_mod = a % p, b_mod = b % p. 3) Compute product = (a_mod * b_mod) % p. 4) Return product. Time complexity is O(1), space is O(1). This connects to the core logic (L2) as each product in the coefficient accumulation uses this operation. Common mistakes include not reducing modulo p before multiplication (which can cause overflow in languages with fixed-size integers), assuming multiplication is associative without modulo reduction, and not handling negative inputs correctly (in some languages, % returns negative remainders). In Python, % always returns non-negative results, but in C++ or Java, you might need ((a%p)+p)%p. This building block is fundamental to many number-theoretic algorithms beyond polynomial multiplication.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Indexing for Polynomial Coefficients
ladders.desc: Accessing and updating array elements representing polynomial coefficients.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves using array indices to represent polynomial coefficients. The intuition is that a polynomial A(x) = a₀ + a₁x + a₂x² + ... + aₙxⁿ can be represented as an array [a₀, a₁, a₂, ..., aₙ] where index i corresponds to the coefficient of xⁱ. This representation allows efficient access and manipulation. The algorithm: 1) Declare an array of size n+1 for a degree n polynomial. 2) To access coefficient of xⁱ, use array[i]. 3) To update coefficient of xⁱ, assign to array[i]. 4) To compute the product polynomial, the coefficient of xᵏ comes from summing products where exponents add to k, so we access poly1[i] and poly2[j] and update result[i+j]. Time complexity for access/update is O(1), space is O(n) for the array. This connects to the building block (L3) as we need to access coefficients to multiply them. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors (e.g., confusing degree with number of coefficients), not initializing arrays properly, and assuming arrays are dynamically sized in all languages. This operation is basic but crucial for understanding how polynomials are represented in computer memory, which is foundational for more advanced algorithms like FFT and NTT.

examples.trace: Degree is 2, so array size is 3. Index 0: coefficient of x⁰ = 3. Index 1: coefficient of x¹ = 4. Index 2: coefficient of x² = 5.

examples.input: Given array [1, 0, 2, 0], what is coefficient of x²?
examples.trace: Array indices: 0->x⁰, 1->x¹, 2->x², 3->x³. So coefficient of x² is at index 2: value 2.

testCases.input: Update array [0,0,0] to set coefficient of x¹ to 4

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Polynomials as Sums of Terms
ladders.desc: Understanding polynomials as mathematical expressions with coefficients and variables.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: a polynomial is an expression consisting of variables (like x) raised to non-negative integer exponents, multiplied by coefficients, and summed together. For example, 3x² + 2x + 1 is a polynomial of degree 2. The intuition is that polynomials generalize linear functions and can represent curves. Key components: 1) Coefficients: numbers multiplying the variables (e.g., 3, 2, 1). 2) Variables: usually x. 3) Exponents: non-negative integers (e.g., 2, 1, 0). 4) Terms: each coefficient-variable-exponent combination (e.g., 3x²). Polynomials are added by combining like terms (same exponent) and multiplied by distributing each term of one polynomial to every term of the other. For example, (x+1)(x+2) = x*x + x*2 + 1*x + 1*2 = x² + 3x + 2. This connects to array indexing (L4) because we can store coefficients in an array where the index represents the exponent. Common mistakes include confusing coefficients with exponents, not understanding that x⁰ = 1, and mishandling negative coefficients. This foundation is essential for all polynomial algorithms, from simple evaluation to advanced multiplication techniques.

examples.output: Degree 3 polynomial with coefficients: 5 for x⁰, 2 for x¹, 0 for x², 4 for x³
examples.trace: Terms: 4x³ (coefficient 4, exponent 3), 2x (coefficient 2, exponent 1), 5 (coefficient 5, exponent 0). Missing x² term means coefficient 0 for exponent 2.
Distribute: x*x = x², x*3 = 3x, 2*x = 2x, 2*3 = 6. Combine like terms: 3x + 2x = 5x. Result: x² + 5x + 6.

testCases.input: What is coefficient of x in 5x³ + 4x + 7?

approaches.name: Divide and Conquer O(n^log₂3)
approaches.summary: Apply the Karatsuba algorithm to recursively split the polynomials into smaller parts, reducing the number of multiplications compared to brute force, and combine results with additions and subtractions before applying modulo operations. This improves performance for moderately sized polynomials.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Polynomial Multiplication mod Prime using Karatsuba
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of polynomial multiplication modulo a prime using Karatsuba's divide-and-conquer algorithm.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires multiplying two polynomials A(x) and B(x) of degree n-1, where coefficients are integers modulo a prime P. The naive O(n²) approach is inefficient for moderate n. Karatsuba's algorithm reduces this to O(n^log₂3) ≈ O(n¹·⁵⁸) by cleverly splitting polynomials. The intuition: Instead of computing all n² coefficient products, we express A = A₁x^m + A₀ and B = B₁x^m + B₀, where m = n/2. Then AB = A₁B₁x²ᵐ + [(A₁+A₀)(B₁+B₀) - A₁B₁ - A₀B₀]xᵐ + A₀B₀. This requires only three multiplications of half-sized polynomials (A₁B₁, A₀B₀, (A₁+A₀)(B₁+B₀)) instead of four. Recursively applying this yields the speedup. Modulo operations are applied after additions/subtractions to keep coefficients in [0, P-1]. Steps: 1) Base case: if n ≤ threshold (e.g., 32), use naive multiplication. 2) Split A and B into high/low halves. 3) Compute three recursive products. 4) Combine with appropriate shifts and modular arithmetic. Time: T(n) = 3T(n/2) + O(n) → O(n^log₂3). Space: O(n) for recursion stack and intermediate arrays. Connection: This is the complete solution using the divide-and-conquer approach. Common mistakes: Forgetting modulo after additions, incorrect handling of odd n, inefficient base case threshold. Real code pattern: def karatsuba(A, B, P): n = len(A); if n <= 32: return naive_mul(A,B,P); m = n//2; A0,A1 = A[:m], A[m:]; B0,B1 = B[:m], B[m:]; Z0 = karatsuba(A0,B0,P); Z2 = karatsuba(A1,B1,P); sumA = [(a+b)%P for a,b in zip(A0,A1)]; sumB = [(a+b)%P for a,b in zip(B0,B1)]; Z1 = karatsuba(sumA,sumB,P); Z1 = [(z1 - z0 - z2) % P for z0,z1,z2 in zip(Z0,Z1,Z2)]; result = [0]*(2*n-1); for i,z in enumerate(Z0): result[i] = (result[i] + z) % P; for i,z in enumerate(Z1): result[i+m] = (result[i+m] + z) % P; for i,z in enumerate(Z2): result[i+2*m] = (result[i+2*m] + z) % P; return result.

examples.trace: n=2, base case threshold=32, use naive: compute 1*3=3, 1*4+2*3=10, 2*4=8, all mod 1000000007 (unchanged). Result: [3,10,8].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Karatsuba Recursive Multiplication
ladders.desc: Implement the core recursive function that multiplies two polynomials using Karatsuba's formula.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the algorithm: given two polynomials A and B (as lists of coefficients) and a prime P, it computes their product C = A * B modulo P using divide-and-conquer. The intuition: By splitting polynomials into halves, we reduce the problem size and exploit the algebraic identity to save one multiplication per recursion. Steps: 1) If n (length of A and B) is small (e.g., ≤32), use naive O(n²) multiplication for efficiency. 2) Otherwise, let m = n//2. Split A into low part A0 (first m coefficients) and high part A1 (last n-m coefficients); similarly for B. 3) Recursively compute Z0 = A0 * B0, Z2 = A1 * B1. 4) Compute sumA = (A0 + A1) mod P and sumB = (B0 + B1) mod P. 5) Recursively compute Z1 = sumA * sumB. 6) Compute Z1 = (Z1 - Z0 - Z2) mod P. 7) Combine: C = Z0 + Z1 shifted by m + Z2 shifted by 2m, applying modulo. Time: O(n^log₂3) as per master theorem. Space: O(n) for recursion depth and temporary arrays. Connection: This function is called by the full solution (L0). Common mistakes: Incorrect splitting for odd n, not applying modulo after additions, off-by-one errors in shifting. Real code pattern: def karatsuba_rec(A, B, P): n = len(A); if n <= 32: return naive_mul(A,B,P); m = n//2; A0,A1 = A[:m], A[m:]; B0,B1 = B[:m], B[m:]; Z0 = karatsuba_rec(A0,B0,P); Z2 = karatsuba_rec(A1,B1,P); sumA = [(a+b)%P for a,b in zip(A0,A1)]; sumB = [(a+b)%P for a,b in zip(B0,B1)]; Z1 = karatsuba_rec(sumA,sumB,P); Z1 = [(z1 - z0 - z2) % P for z0,z1,z2 in zip(Z0,Z1,Z2)]; result = [0]*(2*n-1); for i,z in enumerate(Z0): result[i] = (result[i] + z) % P; for i,z in enumerate(Z1): result[i+m] = (result[i+m] + z) % P; for i,z in enumerate(Z2): result[i+2*m] = (result[i+2*m] + z) % P; return result.

examples.trace: n=2 ≤32, use naive: compute 1*3=3, 1*4+2*3=10, 2*4=8, mod P (unchanged).

ladders.title: Core Logic — Karatsuba's Identity for Polynomial Multiplication
ladders.desc: Understand and apply the algebraic identity that reduces four multiplications to three.
ladders.explanation: The core insight of Karatsuba's algorithm is the identity: For polynomials A = A₁x^m + A₀ and B = B₁x^m + B₀, their product AB = A₁B₁x²ᵐ + [(A₁+A₀)(B₁+B₀) - A₁B₁ - A₀B₀]xᵐ + A₀B₀. Intuition: Normally, computing AB requires four multiplications (A₁B₁, A₁B₀, A₀B₁, A₀B₀). But note that A₁B₀ + A₀B₁ = (A₁+A₀)(B₁+B₀) - A₁B₁ - A₀B₀. Thus, we only need three multiplications: A₁B₁, A₀B₀, and (A₁+A₀)(B₁+B₀). This reduces the work from 4T(n/2) to 3T(n/2), leading to O(n^log₂3) time. Steps: 1) Split A and B into high and low parts. 2) Compute the three products. 3) Combine using the identity. This is applied recursively. Time: Each recursion reduces problem size by half but with three subproblems. Space: O(n) for storing splits and results. Connection: This logic is implemented in the key sub-routine (L1). Common mistakes: Misunderstanding the shift amounts (x^m and x²ᵐ), incorrect sign in the identity. Real code pattern: m = n//2; A0,A1 = A[:m], A[m:]; B0,B1 = B[:m], B[m:]; Z0 = A0*B0; Z2 = A1*B1; sumA = A0+A1; sumB = B0+B1; Z1 = sumA*sumB; Z1 = Z1 - Z0 - Z2; result = Z0 + (Z1 << m) + (Z2 << 2*m).

ladders.title: Building Block — Polynomial Addition and Shifting with Modulo
ladders.desc: Perform coefficient-wise addition and shift operations on polynomials modulo a prime.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves manipulating polynomials as lists of coefficients under modular arithmetic. Key operations: 1) Addition: Given two polynomials A and B of same degree (padded if needed), compute C[i] = (A[i] + B[i]) mod P for each coefficient i. Intuition: Polynomial addition corresponds to adding like terms; modulo ensures coefficients stay within field. 2) Shifting: Multiplying a polynomial by x^k corresponds to shifting its coefficient list right by k positions, i.e., prepending k zeros. For example, if A = [a0,a1], then A*x = [0,a0,a1]. Steps for addition: Ensure equal length by padding with zeros; iterate through indices; add coefficients; apply modulo. Steps for shifting: Create new list of length len(A)+k; copy A to positions starting at index k. Time: O(n) for addition, O(n+k) for shifting. Space: O(n) for new lists. Connection: These operations are used in Karatsuba's identity (L2) to combine results. Common mistakes: Forgetting modulo after addition, incorrect padding for addition, off-by-one in shifting. Real code pattern: def poly_add(A, B, P): n = max(len(A), len(B)); res = [0]*n; for i in range(n): a = A[i] if i < len(A) else 0; b = B[i] if i < len(B) else 0; res[i] = (a + b) % P; return res. def poly_shift(A, k): return [0]*k + A.

examples.trace: Create list of length 3+2=5. Place A starting at index 2: positions 2,3,4 get 1,1,1. Others are 0.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Naive Polynomial Multiplication mod Prime
ladders.desc: Multiply two polynomials using the straightforward O(n²) method with modulo arithmetic.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation computes the product of two polynomials A and B of degrees n-1 and m-1 by directly applying the convolution formula: C[k] = Σ_{i=0}^{k} A[i] * B[k-i] for k from 0 to n+m-2, with each sum taken modulo prime P. Intuition: Each term A[i]xⁱ * B[j]xʲ contributes to coefficient of xⁱ⁺ʲ, so we iterate over all pairs (i,j) and accumulate results. Steps: 1) Initialize result array of length n+m-1 with zeros. 2) For i from 0 to n-1: For j from 0 to m-1: result[i+j] = (result[i+j] + A[i]*B[j]) % P. 3) Return result. Time: O(n*m), which is O(n²) if n=m. Space: O(n+m) for the result. Connection: This is used as the base case in Karatsuba recursion (L1) for small polynomials. Common mistakes: Off-by-one in array lengths, forgetting modulo after multiplication and addition, inefficient double loops. Real code pattern: def naive_mul(A, B, P): n, m = len(A), len(B); res = [0]*(n+m-1); for i in range(n): for j in range(m): res[i+j] = (res[i+j] + A[i]*B[j]) % P; return res.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Polynomial Representation and Modular Arithmetic
ladders.desc: Understand polynomials as lists of coefficients and perform basic modular operations.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept introduces polynomials and modular arithmetic. A polynomial A(x) = a₀ + a₁x + a₂x² + ... + a_{n-1}x^{n-1} is represented as a list of coefficients [a₀, a₁, ..., a_{n-1}], where each aᵢ is an integer. The degree is n-1. Modular arithmetic: For a prime P, we work modulo P, meaning all coefficients are reduced to the range [0, P-1] using the modulo operator %. Key operations: 1) Modulo reduction: Given an integer a, compute a % P. 2) Coefficient access: A[i] gives the coefficient of xⁱ. Intuition: Polynomials are like vectors; modular arithmetic ensures numbers stay manageable and forms a field. Steps for modulo: If a is negative, add P until non-negative; otherwise, compute remainder. Example: For P=5, 7 % 5 = 2, -3 % 5 = 2. Time: O(1) per operation. Space: O(n) to store polynomial. Connection: This is the basis for all higher-level operations (L4). Common mistakes: Confusing degree with length, mishandling negative numbers in modulo. Real code pattern: Represent polynomial as list: A = [1, 2, 3] for 1 + 2x + 3x². Modulo: def mod(a, P): return a % P.

examples.input: Polynomial A = [1, 2, 3], access coefficient at i=1
examples.trace: A[1] corresponds to coefficient of x¹, which is 2.
7 divided by 5 gives remainder 2, so 7 % 5 = 2.
